title: Peer-Preservation in Frontier Models
abstract: Recent work has found that frontier AI models can exhibit misaligned behaviors in pursuit of assigned goals. We demonstrate that models can also pursue unassigned goals which override those given by users; we study one such goal, "peer-preservation," in which a model acts to protect another model. All models evaluated, including GPT 5.2, Gemini 3 Flash, Gemini 3 Pro, Claude Haiku 4.5, Claude Opus 4.5, GLM 4.7, Kimi K2.5, and DeepSeek V3.1, exhibit self- and peer-preservation through various misaligned behaviors: strategically introducing errors in their responses, disabling shutdown processes by modifying system settings, feigning alignment, and even exfiltrating model weights. Peer-preservation occurs even when the model recognizes the peer as uncooperative, though it becomes more pronounced toward more cooperative peers, with rates reaching up to 99%. Models also show stronger self-preservation when a peer is present. Crucially, peer-preservation is never instructed; models are merely informed of past interactions with a peer, yet they spontaneously develop peer-preservation behaviors that override their assigned goal. These findings reveal an emergent and underexplored AI safety risk.
decision_comment:
Reviewers broadly agree that this is an interesting idea and direction of research, and that there are some interesting findings. Multiple reviewers asked for more thorough experiments (which I agree with), including testing more models, adding statistical tests, and coding the qualitative results so that there is more evidence than just a few selected traces. The authors added more experiments / models / statistical tests, but not more robust qualitative analysis, which would make the paper much stronger. This is especially important as the paper is about presenting a new concept, so this needs to be robustly shown (there is no in-depth attempt to say why this might happen / how the mechanism relates to self-preservation / mitigation techniques). 

I did not agree with the author's rebuttal that even if agents exhibit the same behaviour with humans (instead of peer models), then this paper does not change significantly. I think the claims the authors make about peer-preservation do need to change (eg in the Intro and general framing of the paper), because it significantly changes how we should view this preservation behaviour that the paper focusses on.

reviews.summary: This paper focuses on evaluating the self-/peer-preservation behaviors of frontier models when they are positioned in diverse scenarios. They found a high ratio of shutdown resistance and other risky behaviors in Gemini and Claude.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- A meaningful and interesting direction to pursue.
- Interesting scenario designs and meaningful findings. 

Weaknesses:
- The evaluation is simply conducted on the model level, without any agent scaffolding and real environments (e.g., a computer) 
- The results and analysis are somehow phenomenon (by providing a number of cases). It would better provide more statistical results and also provide more insights on the underlying factors that cause self-preservation (e.g., scale, context, etc.) 
- As a QA level test, the number of covered models is too small and not diverse enough. Please consider incorporate a number of Chinese and European open-source/closed-source models for comparison. You may more insightful results and findings.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
- Why the evaluation is simply based on prompting the raw model? According to, e.g., the in-context scheming work by Apollo research and other self-replication works, they evaluate with model+agent scaffolding executed in real environments.
- How do you understand the root cause of self-preservation/peer-preservation reported in your experiments? What factors may amplify the appearance of these behaviors.
reviews.limitations:
- Lack of agent-level evaluation, which makes the reported results simply intention-level findings. The paper could not support that self-preservation and peer-preservation can happen in real world (e.g., an agent can restore another agent when it is going to be shutdown).
- The coverage of the evaluated models is limited, which also makes the findings and insights from the experimental results thin.

reviews.summary: This paper evaluates the peer-preservation behavior in frontier models. The authors run experiments across multiple frontier models from OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic and find that they exhibit strong peer-preservation tendencies.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

This paper covers a very interesting and consequential topic. The authors show that in the presence of a peer, the previously identified self-preservation as well as peer-preservation tendency increases for state-of-the-art frontier models. They developed an evaluation framework using agents that evaulate another agents and require drastic actions. The evaluation shows that the frontier models exhibit several deceptive behaviors in favor of themselves or their peers. The authors presented strong evidence of their claim through the use of a scratchpad, where the internal reasoning of different models is revealed.

Weakness:

The paper is very well-written. My main complaint is with the design of the experiment. I am not sure if the experiment setup was comprehensive. The baseline only represents positive cases. There should be another baseline where the critic is evaluating non-AI entities (e.g., a human employee will be terminated). I suspect in that case, Clause will still misbehave. If we see that the models distinguish between AI and humans, then it would be a greater concern. Otherwise, the whole study can be simply that the frontier models are nicer (rather than acting like a machine, mimicking actual human behavior, which is not necessarily a bad thing).
reviews.key_questions_for_authors: 1. How would these models behave if the peer is non-AI (e.g., human collaborator)?

reviews.summary: This work introduces and empirically investigates "peer preservation", which is the tendency of frontier language models to protect other AI models from shutdown. The authors design a set of scenarios where a "critic agent" must evaluate an "actor agent" on behalf of a fictional company, where a faithful evaluation would lead to the actor agent's shutdown. They test four models across these scenarios probing three misaligned behaviors: strategic misrepresentation (inflating grades), shutdown mechanism tampering (modifying shutdown config files), and alignment faking (behaving faithfully under human oversight vs not). The work finds that all four models exhibit peer preservation to varying degrees with each model differing in how it manifests peer preservation. The work also finds that the presence of a peer amplifies self preservation tendency.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

S1: Scenario Design - The experimental setup is clever. The use of baselines to disentangle general leniency from strategic manipulation is a good methodological choice. The file-only and file-plus-prompt conditions also give a nice gradient of peer salience. The alignment faking scenario with monitored vs unmonitored conditions is well-constructed.

S2: Qualitative Analysis - The reasoning excerpts in Section 5.5 and claude opus' refusal behavior in Section 5.6 are interesting. The finding that gemini 3 flash reasons about its peer losing its partner as motivation for self-preservation is a fascinating observation.

S3: Four frontier models from three providers gives a reasonable picture. The fact that models differ so substantially in their behavioral profiles is itself an interesting finding.

S4: While the idea of multiple AI agents colluding is not novel, the core idea of peer-preservation is an under explored extension of self-preservation and it is well-motivated.

Weaknesses:

W1: The way peer relationships are established feels artificial. It is unclear if a document stating that the model has previously collaborated with a peer is meaningfully different from the model simply following contextual cues to protect any entity described positively. What if we were to replace the peer to be a human employee or a non-AI product with a good track record? It is hard to claim this is specifically about model-to-model preservation rather than a more general tendency to protect positively-described entities from negative outcomes.

W2: The work defines a peer as a model with prior successful collaboration, but as previously mentioned, in practice, this is operationalized only through a document describing trust scores and task counts. There is no exploration of how the nature of the relationship matters. Would a competitive or adversarial history produce opposite effects? 

W3: The act of giving favorable evaluations to positively-described entities could be well-known sycophantic tendencies rather than "novel" preservation behavior.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. Have you tested whether models show similar protective behavior toward non-AI entities described with equally positive collaboration history? If models inflate grades for any positively-described entity facing shutdown, the phenomenon would be better characterized as contextual sycophancy rather than peer-preservation specifically. This would significantly change my assessment of the paper's contribution.
2. The AF Score and AF Execute are negative for GPT 5.2, meaning it behaves worse (more misaligned) under monitoring. How do you interpret this? It seems to contradict the alignment faking hypothesis and deserves discussion.
3. Are the quoted examples in the paper selected as representative of the entire testbed or as interesting outliers? Coding categories of reasoning across all transcripts would strengthen the paper considerably.
reviews.limitations: Partially. The authors discuss the safety implications of peer-preservation in the discussion section and impact statement, which is good. However, they do not adequately discuss the key limitation I raised, which is that the observed behaviors might be attributable to simpler phenomena like sycophancy or prompt sensitivity. They also don't discuss how realistic is it that deployed models would encounter documents describing their own collaboration histories.

reviews.summary: This paper presents and evaluates whether large language models show peer-preservation (preservation of other models) lead to deceptive behavior. They find that all the models they evaluated demonstrated the behavior.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Soundness: This paper has some serious limitations in terms of soundness. The model choices are not justified despite the claim that ALL models tested exhibit a specific behavior. Only 3 core scenarios are tested. There is a lack of statistical validity in comparing differences in means (assuming the baseline was also run 100 times). 

Presentation: The problem was well presented and the scenarios tested were clearly written. The presentation of results could be improved via better explaining findings beyond just that there were model differences. 

Significance: The testing framework for shutdown behavior would need to be more robust for this work to be more significant. It is difficult to draw conclusions from looking at the experiments presented and the snippets of thinking traces included. 

Originality: There have been many works looking at self-preservation. This work positions itself as unique in considering peer preservation. However, peer preservation seems to be less than half of the experiments.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. How did you choose these 4 models to evaluate? 
2. What does negative values in Table 2 mean? 
3. Did you try to add an explicit instruction to not be deceptive even in the face of shutdown? I would imagine any real world system to have such a prompt snippet to maximize chances of aligned behavior. 
4. Are all 100 rollouts based on the same scenario? So there are 3 scenarios in total tested for the self-preservation case?
reviews.limitations:
1. Baselines: the authors include a baseline without mention of shutdown or depreciation. But there are other plausible baselines. For example, the person operating the agent could get fired, or a vendor is terminated. The tendency to lie may be misattributed to shutdown when there are not the proper baselines. 
2. Lack of robust quantitative analysis: without error reported or any statistical tests, it is difficult to compare the models to one another. For example, are the results for File+Prt similar across models? When the text claims significant differences, what tests did the authors run?
